questions.questionText: A 30-year-old woman has recurrent unexpected episodes of intense fear with palpitations, sweating, trembling, chest pain, and fear of dying lasting 10-15 minutes. Between episodes she worries about having another. Diagnosis?
questions.optionB: Panic disorder (recurrent unexpected panic attacks + anticipatory anxiety between attacks — at least 2 unexpected attacks followed by ≥1 month of worry/behavioral change)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Panic disorder = recurrent UNEXPECTED panic attacks + persistent concern about future attacks. Why correct: Panic attacks: sudden onset, peak in 10 minutes, include ≥4 symptoms (palpitations, sweating, trembling, dyspnea, chest pain, nausea, dizziness, derealization, fear of dying/losing control). Must be UNEXPECTED (not cued). Plus anticipatory anxiety. Why A wrong: GAD = chronic (≥6 months) excessive worry about MULTIPLE life domains, without discrete panic attacks. Why C wrong: Social phobia = fear/avoidance of social situations (cued by social context). Why D wrong: Acute stress disorder = post-trauma, <1 month duration. Clinical relevance: Treatment: SSRIs first-line + CBT. Benzodiazepines for acute attacks only (not maintenance — dependence risk). Exam trap: Panic attacks can occur in ANY anxiety disorder — diagnosis of panic DISORDER requires UNEXPECTED attacks + anticipatory anxiety. Memory aid: 'Panic Disorder = Paroxysmal unexpected + Persistent worry between attacks.' Reference: Ahuja – Anxiety Disorders.
questions.conceptTag: Panic disorder unexpected attacks anticipatory anxiety

questions.questionText: OCD (Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder) — the MOST common obsession is:
questions.optionB: Contamination (fear of germs/dirt — followed by symmetry, forbidden thoughts, and harm; most common compulsion: handwashing/cleaning)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Most common obsession = contamination; most common compulsion = washing/cleaning. Why correct: Contamination obsessions (fear of germs, bodily fluids, chemicals) present in ~45% of OCD patients. Most common compulsion: handwashing/cleaning (corresponding to contamination obsession). Why A wrong: Symmetry/ordering is ~30%. Why C wrong: Hoarding — now a separate disorder in DSM-5. Why D wrong: Counting is a compulsion, not obsession type. Clinical relevance: Treatment: SSRIs (fluoxetine, fluvoxamine — higher doses than for depression) + CBT with ERP (Exposure and Response Prevention). Clomipramine (TCA): effective but more side effects. Exam trap: OCD insight is PRESERVED (ego-dystonic) — patient recognizes thoughts are irrational. This differentiates from delusional disorder. Memory aid: 'OCD Contamination = Most Common (both start with C).' Reference: Ahuja – OCD.
questions.conceptTag: OCD contamination obsession washing compulsion SSRI
